text: near the placenta. He wants nothing to do with this world. The doctor tugged again, gently. He prefers it inside. “Get what you can of the cord and clamp it. We need to cut early.” “What’s going on? Is my baby okay?” the mother asked. “Absolutely, Ms. Salyer. We’ve got this.” Hands grabbed the umbilical cord, carefully pulled, then clamped it. The doctor pulled again, but the baby’s insistent grip remained. It’s better in there. Another pull. It’s safe. It’s secure. It’s judgment free and quiet. “Nurse, hold the legs. I need to find out what’s going on in there.” The nurse held the baby’s legs, while Dr. Lipton slipped his arm through the incision up to the elbow. The mother attempted to prop herself up and look beyond the blue drape. “What do you mean? What’s wrong with my baby?” “Ma’am, everything is okay.” Another nurse said. “Just relax. Not every birth is the same.” If the mother could see their faces, she would accurately read confusion. “I need more light,” Dr. Lipton said. The nurse struggled with a pen light, but there were too many arms, too many fingers. “Let me see it.” The doctor held his free hand out. “And place a retractor here.” There was a disturbing urgency in his voice. “Dr. Lipton, perhaps we should just just pull the baby free. I’m sure he’ll—” “He doesn’t want to join us...I need to know why. Now put the retractor in, and give me some room.” The nurses traded glances of unsurety, while the worried mother closed her eyes and mouthed a prayer. Stan’s brow furrowed as he searched the womb, feeling for the baby’s hands, fingering the spongy walls inside. Finally, he carefully pried tiny fingers from the umbilical cord. “Pull,” he commanded. The nurse holding the legs scooped her hand under the baby and carefully pulled. The doctor slid his arm out, and with it the baby’s head. An air of relief filled the room, and the nurse covered the baby with a cloth, wiped it down, and laid it on the mother’s chest. “He’s perfect,” the nurse said. “Yes, he is.” The mother’s sweat-filled face stretched into an impossibly wide smile, as she held her newborn. “Congratulations. I’ll give you two a moment, and then we’ll need to cut the cord closer and get him checked out and cleaned up. In the meantime, the doctor is going to retrieve the placenta, so you should feel a little pressure.” Dr. Lipton stared at the gaping hole in the woman’s abdomen. There was a secret inside that hole, a reason for living, something that offered enlightenment. Like a junkie with its needle, the baby had to be pried from it. The nurse shifted the retractor slightly, a gentle nudge for the doctor to please carry on with the procedure. Stan pushed passed the red flappy doorway with his fingers and drove both hands into the empty chamber, where the baby held its secrets for nine months. He trailed the remainder of the cord back to the placenta, gripped it, and pulled it out, perhaps too quickly, too rough. But his mission wasn’t to finish the Cesarean procedure. It was to inspect the squishy mass, and if no answer was found there, to explore the hole further. He wanted to stay inside. The nurse watched Dr. Lipton as he squeezed the placenta, probing it with his fingers, as though searching for a pearl hidden within. “Dr. Lipton?” “Yes...give me a moment.” He searched for another few seconds, set the placenta aside, then headed back to the bloody hole. Once again, he placed his hand inside the woman, running his fingers across the baby’s vacant home. “There’s something in here,” he whispered. Oblivious to the doctor’s intentions, she waited patiently next to him, ready to assist. But when he brought his face surprisingly close to the open incision, she spoke up. “Doctor. Is everything alright?” “I plan to find out,” he said, then opened the incision wide and buried his face inside, breathing deep from the space the baby wanted to remain. “Dr. Lipton. What are you doing?” The nurse pulled at his shoulder, but he resisted, burying his face deeper still. The mother called out from the other side of the drape. “Please tell me what’s happening. I feel like this whole time you haven’t been keeping me—” She let out a groan. “Is it supposed to feel like this?” The other nurse stayed at her side, assuring her everything was fine, while wide eyes stayed glued to the doctor and his inappropriate quest for truth. “Dr. Lipton!” The nurse tugged hard at his shoulders, while half of his head was submerged. “Angela, get security.” “Security? Why?” the mother asked, holding her baby tight. Angela ran from the room while the other nurse grabbed Dr. Lipton by the hair and yanked his head back. She gasped at the unexpected look of satisfaction on the doctor’s glistening red face—saucer-wide eyes and a toothy grin. “Of course,” he said. “It’s clear to any of us from day one, but...we forget. Over the years...we forget.” Two security guards, sporting belts full of items they never get to use, grabbed the doctor and rushed him away through the doors. “We’ve known it all along,” the doctor said. “But we forget.” As the doctor was pulled through the hall, collecting stares along the way, his suicidal ideation disintegrated, replaced by contentment. And despite the trouble he was in, the grin remained, while remembering the secret we’d forgotten so many years ago. Too often in the horror genre, young writers are compared to Stephen King, with little or no basis for such a comparison. It seems like it's simply the trendy thing to do for reviewers to cry “Behold the next Stephen King!” (Note: Editor's tongue is firmly in-cheek). However, when I read Todd Keisling's epic small-town horror novel Devil's Creek , I couldn't stop thinking how reminiscent it was of King's Salem's
